content:
Everyone says coding is dead. Real engineers know that’s not what is happening.

4 min read
Just now

--

For the past few years, social media has been flooded with one question:
“Will AI replace programmers?”
Open X (formerly Twitter), LinkedIn, YouTube, or any tech podcast, and you’ll find endless debates about the death of software engineering. According to some influencers and executives, coding is already “solved,” developers are becoming obsolete, and AI agents will soon build entire companies by themselves.
But when you step away from the headlines and look at how software is actually built, the reality is very different.
The Rise of AI Anxiety
Many developers are experiencing something new: AI anxiety.
Not because AI tools are useless. In fact, modern AI assistants can be incredibly helpful. They can generate boilerplate code, explain documentation, write tests, and help developers move faster.
The problem is the constant stream of exaggerated claims.
Every week, a new podcast clip goes viral. A founder claims AI will replace engineers within months. A startup announces a revolutionary autonomous coding system. An influencer declares that traditional software development is finished.
These statements generate clicks, engagement, and investment attention.
They don’t always reflect reality.
What Real Software Engineering Looks Like
Building software has never been just about typing code.
Successful software products require:

• Understanding customer needs

• Designing systems

• Making architectural decisions

• Managing security risks

• Debugging production issues

• Reviewing trade-offs

• Maintaining systems for years
AI can assist with many of these tasks.
It cannot magically remove them.
A generated codebase still needs someone to verify it, secure it, maintain it, and improve it over time.
The hard part of software engineering has never been writing characters into a text editor. The hard part is solving problems correctly.
The Metrics Problem
One trend that has become increasingly common is the obsession with productivity metrics.
Companies proudly announce:

• More deployments per day

• More AI-generated code

• More automated workflows

• More agents running simultaneously
But bigger numbers do not automatically mean better outcomes.
Imagine measuring a restaurant solely by how many plates leave the kitchen. That tells you nothing about food quality, customer satisfaction, or profitability.
Software is similar.
The metrics that truly matter are:

• Reliability

• Performance

• Security

• Customer satisfaction

• Business impact
Shipping code faster is valuable only if the software actually works.
When Buzzwords Replace Meaning
Technology has always had buzzwords.
However, the AI era has accelerated this phenomenon dramatically.
Terms like:

• Agentic workflows

• Autonomous systems

• Orchestration

• AI loops

• Multi-agent frameworks

• Context engineering
are often used without clear definitions.
In some cases, these concepts represent real engineering ideas.
In other cases, they are simply marketing language designed to make ordinary automation sound revolutionary.
The result is confusion.
Developers spend more time debating terminology than discussing practical solutions.
AI Is a Tool, Not a Replacement
History shows that powerful tools change jobs rather than eliminate them entirely.
Spreadsheets did not eliminate accountants.
Cameras did not eliminate artists.
Power tools did not eliminate construction workers.
Likewise, AI is unlikely to eliminate software engineers.
Instead, it changes how engineers work.
Developers who learn to effectively use AI tools can automate repetitive tasks and focus more on higher-value work.
The profession evolves, but it does not disappear.
The Real Bottleneck Was Never Coding
One of the biggest misconceptions in technology is that writing code is the primary obstacle to building successful products.
In reality, many startups fail despite having functioning software.
The real challenges are:

• Finding a market

• Understanding users

• Creating value

• Building sustainable businesses

• Executing consistently
AI can generate code.
It cannot automatically create great ideas, strong leadership, or successful products.
Those remain human responsibilities.
Why Developers Should Stay Optimistic
Despite all the noise online, software engineering remains one of the most valuable skills in the modern economy.
Businesses still need:

• Web applications

• Mobile apps

• Cloud infrastructure

• Data systems

• Security solutions

• Automation tools
AI increases demand for software in many areas because it enables companies to build more products faster.
Someone still needs to design, manage, secure, and improve those systems.
That someone is usually an engineer.
The Future of Programming
The future of software development will likely look very different from the past.
Developers will spend less time writing repetitive code and more time:

• Reviewing AI-generated solutions

• Defining requirements

• Designing architectures

• Managing complex systems

• Solving business problems
In other words, programming becomes more strategic rather than disappearing.
The keyboard is not going away.
The job is evolving.
Final Thoughts
The internet loves extreme predictions.
“AI will replace everyone.”
“Programming is dead.”
“One-click startups are the future.”
These statements generate attention because they are dramatic.
Reality is usually less exciting and far more practical.
AI is one of the most important technological advances of our generation. It is already making developers more productive and changing how software is built.
But software engineering is much bigger than writing code.
As long as businesses have problems to solve, customers to serve, and systems to maintain, skilled engineers will remain essential.
The future belongs not to those who fear AI, but to those who learn how to work alongside it.
